title: Romani Anger Stories the West Erased
dek: You were told your anger was a curse, a stereotype, a danger to polite society. But the fury in your jaw and the heat in your psoas are ancient messengers, carrying the erased stories of a people who survived by listening to the body.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Weight in the Jaw

You wake up at 3:14 AM, and the first thing you notice is the ache in your masseter muscle. It is a dense, fibrous knot of tension, a physical manifestation of words swallowed and screams suppressed. This is not a metaphor. This is biology. The jaw remembers what the mind tries to forget. When they tell you that your anger is inappropriate, that it is a stereotype, that it is dangerous, your body absorbs that lie and stores it in the fascia of your face and neck. 

The Romani people have carried a specific, heavy anger for centuries. It is an anger born of erasure, of being pushed to the margins, of having our stories rewritten by those who fear our survival. This anger is not a moral failure. It is a profound, somatic messenger. It tells you that a boundary has been crossed, that a truth has been denied. When you feel that heat rising in your chest, when your breath becomes shallow and your heart hammers against your ribs, that is your body demanding justice.

Bessel van der Kolk teaches us that the body keeps the score. The trauma of erasure is not just a historical footnote; it is a living, breathing reality in our nervous systems. The hypervigilance you feel when you walk into a room where you are the only one of your kind is not paranoia. It is your amygdala doing its job, scanning for threats based on generations of data. Your body is trying to keep you safe.

## The Psoas and the Flight Response

Consider the psoas muscle, deep in your core, connecting your spine to your legs. It is the muscle of flight, the muscle that prepares you to run when danger approaches. For many of us, the psoas is chronically tight, locked in a state of perpetual readiness. We are always preparing to flee, because history has taught us that staying is dangerous. 

When you try to relax, when you try to meditate or breathe deeply, and you feel a sudden, sharp panic in your gut, that is your psoas speaking. It is saying, "Do not let your guard down. They are coming." This is where the [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be a profound ally. It is not a magic cure, but it is a deep, grounding medicine that speaks directly to the adrenal glands, whispering to the nervous system that it is safe to rest, even if just for a moment.

Resmaa Menakem writes about somatic abolitionism, the idea that trauma is stored in the body and must be healed in the body. We cannot simply think our way out of centuries of oppression. We must feel our way out. We must allow the body to process the anger, the grief, the terror that has been locked in our tissues. This means sitting with the discomfort, noticing the tension in the levator scapulae, the tightness in the diaphragm, and breathing into it without judgment.

## The Fire in the Belly

Anger is often described as a fire, and in Traditional Chinese Medicine, this fire is associated with the liver. When anger is suppressed, the liver qi stagnates, leading to physical symptoms: headaches, digestive issues, menstrual irregularities. Your body is screaming at you to let the fire out. 

But how do we express this fire when the world tells us that our anger is ugly? We start by acknowledging it. We start by feeling the heat in our bellies, the clenching of our fists. We do not judge it. We simply observe it. We say to ourselves, "I am angry, and my anger is valid. My anger is a necessary response to an unjust world."

Gabor Maté reminds us that the suppression of healthy anger is a primary driver of chronic illness. When we turn our anger inward, it attacks our own tissues. It becomes autoimmune disease, chronic pain, depression. The [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help soothe the nervous system when the fire becomes too intense, allowing us to process the anger without being consumed by it. It is a bitter medicine, but it is a necessary one.

## The Practice of Somatic Release

Here is a short embodied practice for when the anger feels too big to hold. 

Find a quiet space where you will not be disturbed. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. Feel the ground beneath your feet. Notice the weight of your body pressing into the earth. 

Bring your attention to your jaw. Notice the tension there. Slowly, deliberately, open your mouth as wide as you can. Stretch the masseter muscle. You may feel a urge to yawn, or to sigh, or to make a sound. Allow it. 

Now, bring your attention to your hands. Clench them into fists, as tight as you can. Feel the tension in your forearms, your biceps, your shoulders. Hold this tension for a count of five. Then, release. Let your hands drop to your sides. Notice the rush of blood, the tingling sensation in your fingers. 

Repeat this process with your core, your glutes, your legs. Tense, hold, release. This is not about getting rid of the anger. It is about moving it through the body, allowing it to complete its cycle. 

## The Erasure of Our Stories

The West has always been uncomfortable with Romani anger. They prefer us as romanticized wanderers, or as tragic victims. They do not want us as angry, empowered individuals demanding our rightful place in the world. Our anger disrupts their narrative. 

When we express our anger, we are often met with gaslighting. We are told that we are overreacting, that we are being unreasonable, that we are playing the victim. This is a deliberate tactic of erasure. It is designed to make us doubt our own reality, to make us question the validity of our own somatic experiences. 

But the body does not lie. The tightness in your chest when you are spoken over, the heat in your face when your history is denied—these are truths that cannot be debated. They are biological facts. And they are the foundation of our resistance.

## Reclaiming Our Fury

To reclaim our anger is to reclaim our humanity. It is to say, "I exist, and I will not be erased." It is a radical act of self-love. 

We must learn to listen to our bodies, to honor the messages they are sending us. We must learn to differentiate between the clean, sharp anger of a boundary violation, and the muddy, toxic anger of internalized oppression. The [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be a valuable tool in this process, helping us navigate the complex landscape of our nervous systems.

Our anger is not a curse. It is a compass. It points us toward justice, toward healing, toward a world where our stories are no longer erased. 

## FAQ

**Q: Is it safe to feel my anger?**
**A:** Yes, but it can be overwhelming. Start small. Notice the physical sensations of anger in your body—the heat, the tension, the shallow breathing. You do not have to act on the anger immediately. Just allow yourself to feel it. If it becomes too much, ground yourself by feeling your feet on the floor or focusing on your breath.

**Q: What if my anger makes other people uncomfortable?**
**A:** Your anger is not responsible for other people's comfort. If your anger is a valid response to a boundary violation or an injustice, then their discomfort is theirs to manage. Do not shrink yourself to make others feel safe. 

**Q: How do I know if my anger is healthy or toxic?**
**A:** Healthy anger is a response to a present-moment threat or violation. It is sharp, clear, and mobilizing. Toxic anger is often rooted in past trauma and is characterized by rumination, resentment, and a desire for revenge. Somatic practices can help you differentiate between the two by bringing your awareness to the physical sensations associated with each.

**Q: Can herbs really help with anger?**
**A:** Herbs are not a substitute for doing the deep somatic work, but they can be powerful allies. Nervines like chamomile and valerian can help soothe a hyperactive nervous system, making it easier to process difficult emotions. Always consult with a qualified herbalist or healthcare practitioner before starting any new herbal protocol.
